title: Service Latency Seasonality and Anomaly (MLTK)
app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Observability Cloud (optional)
spl:
index=main sourcetype=access_combined
| eval response_ms=response_time*1000
| bin _time span=5m
| stats p95(response_ms) as p95_latency, p99(response_ms) as p99_latency, count by _time, uri_path
| eval hour_of_week=(tonumber(strftime(_time,"%w"))*24) + tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))
| fit DensityFunction p95_latency p99_latency by uri_path into latency_seasonal_model
| rename "IsOutlier(p95_latency)" as is_p95_outlier, "IsOutlier(p99_latency)" as is_p99_outlier
| where is_p95_outlier > 0 OR is_p99_outlier > 0
| table _time, uri_path, p95_latency, p99_latency, hour_of_week, count
| sort -p95_latency
description: Application response times follow predictable patterns — higher during business hours, lower at night. By decomposing latency into seasonal (hour-of-week) and residual components, this detection flags abnormal slowdowns that coincide with deployments, infrastructure changes, or emerging incidents — even when raw latency stays within static thresholds.
value: Application response times follow predictable patterns — higher during business hours, lower at night. By decomposing latency into seasonal (hour-of-week) and residual components, this detection flags abnormal slowdowns that coincide with deployments, infrastructure changes, or emerging incidents — even when raw latency stays within static thresholds.
implementation: Collect p95 and p99 latency per endpoint in 5-minute bins. Train DensityFunction models per `uri_path` that learn hour-of-week seasonality. Anomalies represent latency that is unusual for that specific time window, not just above a flat threshold. Correlate with deployment events from CI/CD pipelines (cat-12) and infrastructure changes. Create ITSI KPIs from the anomaly output for service health scoring. Alert application owners via Splunk On-Call with endpoint-specific context. Retrain models weekly.
visualization: Line chart (p95 latency with seasonal overlay), Heatmap (endpoint × hour-of-week), Table (anomalous endpoints).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Observability Cloud (optional).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=main sourcetype=access_combined` or APM traces, `index=o11y sourcetype=otel:metrics`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Collect p95 and p99 latency per endpoint in 5-minute bins. Train DensityFunction models per `uri_path` that learn hour-of-week seasonality. Anomalies represent latency that is unusual for that specific time window, not just above a flat threshold. Correlate with deployment events from CI/CD pipelines (cat-12) and infrastructure changes. Create ITSI KPIs from the anomaly output for service health scoring. Alert application owners via Splunk On-Call with endpoint-specific context. Retrain models w…

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=main sourcetype=access_combined
| eval response_ms=response_time*1000
| bin _time span=5m
| stats p95(response_ms) as p95_latency, p99(response_ms) as p99_latency, count by _time, uri_path
| eval hour_of_week=(tonumber(strftime(_time,"%w"))*24) + tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))
| fit DensityFunction p95_latency p99_latency by uri_path into latency_seasonal_model
| rename "IsOutlier(p95_latency)" as is_p95_outlier, "IsOutlier(p99_latency)" as is_p99_outlier
| where is_p95_outlier > 0 OR is_p99_outlier > 0
| table _time, uri_path, p95_latency, p99_latency, hour_of_week, count
| sort -p95_latency
```

Understanding this SPL

**Service Latency Seasonality and Anomaly (MLTK)** — Application response times follow predictable patterns — higher during business hours, lower at night. By decomposing latency into seasonal (hour-of-week) and residual components, this detection flags abnormal slowdowns that coincide with deployments, infrastructure changes, or emerging incidents — even when raw latency stays within static thresholds.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=main sourcetype=access_combined` or APM traces, `index=o11y sourcetype=otel:metrics`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Observability Cloud (optional). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: main; **sourcetype**: access_combined. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=main, sourcetype=access_combined. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **response_ms** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, uri_path** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hour_of_week** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Pipeline stage (see **Service Latency Seasonality and Anomaly (MLTK)**): fit DensityFunction p95_latency p99_latency by uri_path into latency_seasonal_model
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Filters the current rows with `where is_p95_outlier > 0 OR is_p99_outlier > 0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Service Latency Seasonality and Anomaly (MLTK)**): table _time, uri_path, p95_latency, p99_latency, hour_of_week, count
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.



Step 3 — Validate
Run the search across business and off-peak hours. Compare the anomalies with the APM UI, route-level latency in Grafana, and the same p95 or p99 views you use in production.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (p95 latency with seasonal overlay), Heatmap (endpoint × hour-of-week), Table (anomalous endpoints).
grandmaExplanation: We watch how long work takes across services so slowdowns in our code or a dependency show up while they are still small.
knownFalsePositives: Latency spikes during pool exhaustion, garbage collection, or upstream slowness. We compare with the APM UI and deploy history before a code-only theory.
